passage: Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.
author: Leo Tolstoy
principle_pt: Frase de abertura como tese — declarar um axioma e usar o romance inteiro pra testá-lo.

passage: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.
author: Jane Austen
principle_pt: Ironia montada já na primeira linha: o narrador finge concordar com a sociedade que vai dissecar.

passage: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair.
author: Charles Dickens
principle_pt: Ritmo construído por anáfora e paralelismo — frase longa que ganha urgência pela repetição.

passage: Call me Ishmael.
author: Herman Melville
principle_pt: Frase curta de três palavras estabelece narrador, distância e mistério: por que 'Call me' e não 'My name is'?

passage: As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a gigantic insect.
author: Franz Kafka
principle_pt: Começar dentro da cena, tarde — o evento extraordinário já aconteceu, e o tom é de relato burocrático.

passage: On an exceptionally hot evening early in July a young man came out of the garret in which he lodged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in hesitation, towards K. bridge.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
principle_pt: Detalhe específico (calor de julho, sótão, hesitação) constrói atmosfera psicológica sem explicar.

passage: The Nellie, a cruising yawl, swung to her anchor without a flutter of the sails, and was at rest. The flood had made, the wind was nearly calm, and being bound down the river, the only thing for it was to come to and wait for the turn of the tide.
author: Joseph Conrad
principle_pt: Atmosfera de espera e imobilidade já no primeiro parágrafo — o ambiente prepara o tom da narrativa-moldura.

passage: His soul swooned slowly as he heard the snow falling faintly through the universe and faintly falling, like the descent of their last end, upon all the living and the dead.
author: James Joyce
principle_pt: Epifania final pelo ritmo: pares (faintly falling / falling faintly), aliteração, cadência longa que abre o conto pro universal.

passage: Gazing up into the darkness I saw myself as a creature driven and derided by vanity; and my eyes burned with anguish and anger.
author: James Joyce
principle_pt: Interioridade que vira julgamento — o narrador adolescente se vê de fora, e a vergonha é mostrada pela imagem física dos olhos queimando.

passage: It was said that a new person had appeared on the sea-front: a lady with a little dog. Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov, who had by then been a fortnight at Yalta, and so was fairly at home there, had begun to take an interest in new arrivals.
author: Anton Chekhov
principle_pt: Voz de boato — 'it was said' — distância narrativa que finge ser fofoca antes de aproximar do drama íntimo.

passage:
'They look like white elephants,' she said.
'I've never seen one,' the man drank his beer.
'No, you wouldn't have.'
'I might have,' the man said. 'Just because you say I wouldn't have doesn't prove anything.'
author: Ernest Hemingway
principle_pt: Subtexto puro: nenhum dos dois nomeia o assunto real (aborto), e o conflito vive no que NÃO é dito.

passage: None of them knew the color of the sky. Their eyes glanced level, and were fastened upon the waves that swept toward them.
author: Stephen Crane
principle_pt: POV coletivo em frase curta — em quatro náufragos, ninguém olhou pra cima. O detalhe sensorial define o ponto de vista físico.

passage: A green and yellow parrot, which hung in a cage outside the door, kept repeating over and over: 'Allez vous-en! Allez vous-en! Sapristi! That's all right!'
author: Kate Chopin
principle_pt: Detalhe específico bizarro abrindo o romance — o papagaio mandando ir embora é símbolo e ruído ambiente ao mesmo tempo.

passage: TRUE!—nervous—very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that I am mad?
author: Edgar Allan Poe
principle_pt: Narrador não confiável apresentado em duas linhas: protesto demais, pontuação fragmentada, pergunta retórica defensiva.

passage: 'I would prefer not to.'
author: Herman Melville
principle_pt: Diálogo que é o conto inteiro — uma frase que recusa sem explicar, e o personagem se define pela repetição dela.

passage: 1801.—I have just returned from a visit to my landlord—the solitary neighbour that I shall be troubled with. This is certainly a beautiful country!
author: Emily Brontë
principle_pt: Narrador-moldura ingênuo: ele acha tudo charmoso, e nós vamos descobrir junto que está completamente enganado.

passage: ...for the growing good of the world is partly dependent on unhistoric acts; and that things are not so ill with you and me as they might have been, is half owing to the number who lived faithfully a hidden life, and rest in unvisited tombs.
author: George Eliot
principle_pt: Final que generaliza pelo ritmo — frase longa em três tempos que dilata o particular pro universal.

passage: Marley was dead, to begin with. There is no doubt whatever about that. The register of his burial was signed by the clergyman, the clerk, the undertaker, and the chief mourner. Scrooge signed it: and Scrooge's name was good upon 'Change, for anything he chose to put his hand to. Old Marley was as dead as a door-nail.
author: Charles Dickens
principle_pt: Voz de contador de história — frases curtas, aparte com o leitor, repetição cômica do fato central.

passage: She was one of those pretty and charming girls born, as though fate had blundered over her, into a family of artisans. She had no dowry, no expectations, no means of being known, understood, loved, married by any rich and distinguished man; and she let herself be married to a little clerk at the Ministry of Public Instruction.
author: Guy de Maupassant
principle_pt: Apresentação de personagem em parágrafo único: condição social, frustração, e a vontade já implícita.

passage: Upon the half decayed veranda of a small frame house that stood near the edge of a ravine near the town of Winesburg, Ohio, a fat little old man walked nervously up and down.
author: Sherwood Anderson
principle_pt: Detalhe específico de lugar (veranda meio podre, ravina) prepara o tom melancólico antes de o conflito aparecer.

passage: Selden paused in surprise. In the afternoon rush of the Grand Central Station his eyes had been refreshed by the sight of Miss Lily Bart.
author: Edith Wharton
principle_pt: POV em close third — começar pela percepção de Selden, e a personagem-título entra através do olhar dele.

passage: I first heard of Ántonia on what seemed to me an interminable journey across the great midland plain of North America.
author: Willa Cather
principle_pt: Memória como motor narrativo — 'first heard of' avisa que o que vem é reconstrução, não testemunho direto.

passage: 'My aunt will be down presently, Mr. Nuttel,' said a very self-possessed young lady of fifteen; 'in the meantime you must try and put up with me.'
author: Saki (H. H. Munro)
principle_pt: Diálogo já caracteriza: a expressão 'self-possessed' antes da frase e o tom de quem manda no espaço dizem tudo da menina.

passage: During an interval in the Melvinski trial in the large building of the Law Courts, the members and public prosecutor met in Ivan Egorovich Shebek's private room, where the conversation turned on the celebrated Krasovski case.
author: Leo Tolstoy
principle_pt: Começar pela conversa banal dos colegas — Ivan já está morto, e a indiferença dos vivos vira o tema.

passage: He had a way of writing down his thoughts which betrayed a soul averse from violent emotions. He kept a journal, in which were entered all his innocent expenditures down to the last sou.
author: Gustave Flaubert
principle_pt: Caracterização por hábito mensurável — anotar gastos até o último centavo desenha o sujeito mais do que adjetivos.

passage: You don't know about me without you have read a book by the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer; but that ain't no matter. That book was made by Mr. Mark Twain, and he told the truth, mainly. There was things which he stretched, but mainly he told the truth.
author: Mark Twain
principle_pt: Voz/dialeto desde a primeira frase — sintaxe oral ('without you have read'), pacto direto com o leitor sobre verdade e ficção.

passage: Fog everywhere. Fog up the river, where it flows among green aits and meadows; fog down the river, where it rolls defiled among the tiers of shipping and the waterside pollutions of a great (and dirty) city. Fog on the Essex marshes, fog on the Kentish heights.
author: Charles Dickens
principle_pt: Anáfora obsessiva ('Fog…') faz a paisagem virar personagem opressivo antes de qualquer ação humana.

passage: Mr. Utterson the lawyer was a man of a rugged countenance, that was never lighted by a smile; cold, scanty and embarrassed in discourse; backward in sentiment; lean, long, dusty, dreary, and yet somehow lovable.
source: Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
author: Robert Louis Stevenson
principle_pt: Lista de adjetivos negativos que termina em paradoxo ('and yet somehow lovable') — a virada salva o personagem do estereótipo.

passage: The studio was filled with the rich odour of roses, and when the light summer wind stirred amidst the trees of the garden, there came through the open door the heavy scent of the lilac, or the more delicate perfume of the pink-flowering thorn.
author: Oscar Wilde
principle_pt: Sensação antes de personagem — o cheiro de rosas, lilás e espinhos floridos prepara o decadentismo do romance.

passage: There was no possibility of taking a walk that day. We had been wandering, indeed, in the leafless shrubbery an hour in the morning; but since dinner (Mrs. Reed, when there was no company, dined early) the cold winter wind had brought with it clouds so sombre, and a rain so penetrating, that further out-door exercise was now out of the question.
author: Charlotte Brontë
principle_pt: POV infantil — frio, chuva, casa fechada com Mrs. Reed; o mundo restringido da criança aparece como dado, não como queixa.

passage: To Sherlock Holmes she is always the woman. I have seldom heard him mention her under any other name. In his eyes she eclipses and predominates the whole of her sex.
author: Arthur Conan Doyle
principle_pt: Voz de Watson — narrador que admira e observa de longe; abre com 'always' pra marcar que o caso virou referência.

passage: 3 May. Bistritz.—Left Munich at 8:35 P.M., on 1st May, arriving at Vienna early next morning; should have arrived at 6:46, but train was an hour late. Buda-Pesth seems a wonderful place, from the glimpse which I got of it from the train and the little I could walk through the streets.
author: Bram Stoker
principle_pt: Diário como forma — datas, horários, observações neutras criam a sensação documental que o sobrenatural depois vai contaminar.

passage: On an evening in the latter part of May a middle-aged man was walking homeward from Shaston to the village of Marlott, in the adjoining Vale of Blakemore, or Blackmoor.
author: Thomas Hardy
principle_pt: Pastoral com gancho geográfico — o nome duplo do vale (Blakemore/Blackmoor) já avisa que o lugar tem duas versões, uma luminosa e uma sombria.

passage: And here I have lamely related to you the uneventful chronicle of two foolish children in a flat who most unwisely sacrificed for each other the greatest treasures of their house. But in a last word to the wise of these days let it be said that of all who give gifts these two were the wisest. Of all who give and receive gifts, such as they are wisest. Everywhere they are wisest. They are the magi.
author: O. Henry
principle_pt: Reversão final — chama os personagens de 'foolish' e termina coroando-os; o narrador conduz o leitor da ironia pra reverência em três frases.

passage: Young Goodman Brown came forth at sunset into the street at Salem village; but put his head back, after crossing the threshold, to exchange a parting kiss with his young wife. And Faith, as the wife was aptly named, thrust her own pretty head into the street, letting the wind play with the pink ribbons of her cap while she called to Goodman Brown.
author: Nathaniel Hawthorne
principle_pt: Símbolo plantado na primeira página: a esposa chama-se 'Faith' (Fé) e usa fitas rosa — a partida do marido vira alegoria do abandono da fé.

passage: The cold passed reluctantly from the earth, and the retiring fogs revealed an army stretched out on the hills, resting. As the landscape changed from brown to green, the army awakened, and began to tremble with eagerness at the noise of rumors.
author: Stephen Crane
principle_pt: Exército como criatura única que 'tremble', 'awakened' — a metáfora do corpo coletivo entra antes de qualquer soldado individual.

passage: But I noticed in the dust on the road a small clump of the Tatar plant in flower, called by us a thistle... Two of its three stems were broken and the stump of a torn-off branch protruded; but the third stem still stuck up, although it too was soiled with black mud.
author: Leo Tolstoy
principle_pt: Imagem-frame: o cardo quebrado mas teimoso vira a metáfora explícita do guerreiro que o romance vai narrar.

passage: I am a sick man.... I am a spiteful man. I am an unattractive man. I believe my liver is diseased.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
principle_pt: Quatro frases breves em primeira pessoa: confissão escalando do físico ao moral. O narrador se constrói por autoacusação.

passage: The whole sky had been overcast with rain-clouds from early morning; it was a still day, not hot, but heavy, as it is in grey dull weather when the clouds have been hanging over the country for a long while, when one expects rain and it does not come.
author: Anton Chekhov
principle_pt: Clima de espera — céu pesado, chuva que não vem. O parágrafo é puro estado emocional vestido de meteorologia.

passage: There was no hope for him this time: it was the third stroke. Night after night I had passed the house (it was vacation time) and studied the lighted square of window: and night after night I had found it lighted in the same way, faintly and evenly.
author: James Joyce
principle_pt: Voz infantil que entende o adulto: 'no hope for him this time' soa como adulto reproduzindo um ouvido, e os 'night after night' marcam o tempo lento da criança que observa.
